ucs.title: [LLM] IndigoZebra BoxCaon/xCaon C2 via Dropbox API abuse from Office-spawned process
ucs.description: Hunts IndigoZebra's signature BoxCaon implant behavior: a child of an Office product or a freshly-dropped binary in a user-writable path beaconing to api[.]dropboxapi[.]com / content[.]dropboxapi[.]com using the Bearer-token OAuth pattern. This is the defining tradecraft observed by Check Point in the 2021 Afghan National Security Council campaign.
ucs.splunk:
| tstats summariesonly=true count min(_time) as firstTime max(_time) as lastTime values(Processes.process) as process values(Processes.process_path) as process_path from datamodel=Endpoint.Processes where Processes.parent_process_name IN ("winword.exe","excel.exe","powerpnt.exe","outlook.exe") OR (Processes.process_path IN ("*\\AppData\\Roaming\\Microsoft\\*","*\\AppData\\Local\\Temp\\*","*\\ProgramData\\*") AND Processes.process_name IN ("*.exe","rundll32.exe","regsvr32.exe")) by host Processes.user Processes.process_name Processes.process_id Processes.parent_process_name _time 
| `drop_dm_object_name(Processes)` 
| join type=inner host process_id [ | tstats summariesonly=true count values(All_Traffic.dest) as dest values(All_Traffic.url) as url from datamodel=Network_Traffic.All_Traffic where (All_Traffic.dest IN ("api.dropboxapi.com","content.dropboxapi.com","api-content.dropbox.com")) by host All_Traffic.process_id All_Traffic.bytes_out All_Traffic.bytes_in | rename All_Traffic.process_id as process_id ] 
| stats count values(process_name) as process_name values(parent_process_name) as parent values(dest) as dest sum(bytes_out) as bytes_out by host user 
| where count >= 3 
| `security_content_ctime(firstTime)` | `security_content_ctime(lastTime)`
ucs.kql:
let dropboxC2 = dynamic(["api.dropboxapi.com","content.dropboxapi.com","api-content.dropbox.com"]);
let officeParents = dynamic(["winword.exe","excel.exe","powerpnt.exe","outlook.exe"]);
let suspiciousChildren = DeviceProcessEvents
| where Timestamp > ago(7d)
| where (InitiatingProcessFileName in~ (officeParents))
     or (FolderPath has_any (@"\AppData\Roaming\", @"\AppData\Local\Temp\", @"\ProgramData\")
         and FileName endswith ".exe")
| project Timestamp, DeviceId, DeviceName, AccountName, ChildProcId=ProcessId, ChildName=FileName, ChildPath=FolderPath, InitiatingProcessFileName, InitiatingProcessCommandLine;
let dropboxBeacons = DeviceNetworkEvents
| where Timestamp > ago(7d)
| where RemoteUrl has_any (dropboxC2) or RemoteIPType == "Public" and RemoteUrl endswith "dropboxapi.com"
| project Timestamp, DeviceId, DeviceName, InitiatingProcessId, InitiatingProcessFileName, InitiatingProcessFolderPath, RemoteUrl, RemoteIP;
suspiciousChildren
| join kind=inner (dropboxBeacons) on DeviceId
| where ChildProcId == InitiatingProcessId or InitiatingProcessFolderPath =~ ChildPath
| summarize Beacons=count(), FirstSeen=min(Timestamp1), LastSeen=max(Timestamp1), URLs=make_set(RemoteUrl,10) by DeviceName, AccountName, ChildName, ChildPath, InitiatingProcessFileName
| where Beacons >= 3
ucs.rationale: BoxCaon and the xCaon variant Check Point analysed in the July 2021 'IndigoZebra' campaign exclusively use the Dropbox public REST API (api.dropboxapi.com / content.dropboxapi.com) for both tasking and exfil — a deliberate choice to blend with sanctioned cloud traffic in Central Asian government networks. Most enterprise endpoints have zero legitimate native-process traffic to those FQDNs (the Dro

ucs.title: [LLM] IndigoZebra spearphish: lookalike government-domain sender + RAR/SCR payload chain
ucs.description: Detects the IndigoZebra delivery pattern reported by Check Point: spoofed ministry-of-foreign-affairs themed mail carrying a password-protected RAR that drops an executable masquerading as a document (.scr, double-extension, or LNK). Anchored to Central Asian government victimology and the actor's habit of registering MFA/government-impersonation domains (T1583.001) and using compromised gov mailboxes (T1586.002).
ucs.splunk:
| tstats summariesonly=true count min(_time) as firstTime max(_time) as lastTime values(All_Email.subject) as subject values(All_Email.file_name) as attachment values(All_Email.src_user) as sender from datamodel=Email.All_Email where All_Email.file_name IN ("*.rar","*.zip","*.lnk") AND (match(All_Email.src_user,"(?i)(mfa|mofa|gov|president|nsc|embassy)[-.]?[a-z]{0,4}\.(com|info|net|org|xyz|cc)") OR match(All_Email.subject,"(?i)(meeting|protocol|note verbale|diplomatic|agenda|covid|vaccine).{0,40}(afghan|uzbek|kyrgyz|kazakh|tajik|turkmen|belarus)")) by All_Email.recipient All_Email.message_id 
| `drop_dm_object_name(All_Email)` 
| join type=outer message_id [ | tstats summariesonly=true count values(Filesystem.file_name) as dropped values(Filesystem.file_path) as drop_path from datamodel=Endpoint.Filesystem where Filesystem.file_name IN ("*.scr","*.exe.lnk","*.pdf.exe","*.docx.exe") by Filesystem.dest Filesystem.user | rename Filesystem.dest as dest ] 
| stats values(sender) as sender values(subject) as subject values(attachment) as attachment values(dropped) as dropped_payload count by recipient 
| where count > 0
ucs.kql:
let centralAsiaLures = @"(?i)(afghan|uzbek|kyrgyz|kazakh|tajik|turkmen|belarus|mfa|mofa|nsc|embassy|note\s*verbale|diplomatic\s*protocol)";
let impersonationDomains = @"(?i)(mfa|mofa|gov|president|nsc|embassy)[-.]?[a-z]{0,4}\.(com|info|net|org|xyz|cc)";
let emails = EmailEvents
| where Timestamp > ago(14d)
| where DeliveryAction in ("Delivered","DeliveredAsSpam")
| where SenderFromAddress matches regex impersonationDomains
     or Subject matches regex centralAsiaLures;
let attach = EmailAttachmentInfo
| where Timestamp > ago(14d)
| where FileName endswith ".rar" or FileName endswith ".zip" or FileName endswith ".lnk"
     or FileName matches regex @"\.(pdf|docx|xlsx)\.(exe|scr|lnk)$";
let phish = emails
| join kind=inner attach on NetworkMessageId
| project NetworkMessageId, Timestamp, RecipientEmailAddress, SenderFromAddress, Subject, FileName, SHA256;
let badFiles = DeviceFileEvents
| where Timestamp > ago(14d)
| where FileName endswith ".scr"
     or FileName matches regex @"\.(pdf|docx|xlsx)\.(exe|scr|lnk)$"
     or FolderPath has_any (@"\AppData\Local\Temp\Rar$", @"\AppData\Local\Temp\7z");
phish
| join kind=leftouter (badFiles | project DeviceName, InitiatingProcessAccountName, FileName, FolderPath, SHA256_drop=SHA256, DropTime=Timestamp) on $left.SHA256 == $right.SHA256_drop
| extend ChainObserved = isnotempty(SHA256_drop)
| project Timestamp, RecipientEmailAddress, SenderFromAddress, Subject, FileName, SHA256, DeviceName, FolderPath, ChainObserved
| order by Timestamp desc
ucs.rationale: Check Point's IndigoZebra/'BoxCaon' report and the Securelist 2017 APT trends note the same operational pattern: registering domains spoofing the targeted state's MFA/Presidential office, sending diplomatic-themed lures to Central Asian government recipients, and shipping a RAR archive that drops a double-extension executable. Combining the impersonation-regex on the sender FQDN with the regional/
